items.title: Take Care of America’s Veterans Act (Rep. Bost – Veterans' Affairs) (Subject to a Rule)
items.rawText: H.R. 9237 – Take Care of America’s Veterans Act (Rep. Bost – Veterans' Affairs) (Subject to a Rule) – VOTE NO

data.sourceText:
“One Minutes” (5 per side)

H.R. 9237 – Take Care of America’s Veterans Act (Rep. Bost – Veterans' Affairs) (Subject to a Rule) – VOTE NO

This bill purports to increase benefits for veterans and improve services offered by the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA). In reality, it would undercut critical care and benefits for those who have dedicated their lives to serving this country. 

House Republicans have chosen to put forward a bill with no input or buy-in from House Democrats that hijacks bipartisan priorities while advancing their own partisan policy provisions. These provisions will undercut the VA care and benefits veterans rely on day-to-day. While House Democrats are working to ensure veterans have easy access to their disability benefits and treatment options, cutting certain benefits for one group of veterans to fund new benefits for another is not the answer. Unfortunately, this bill does exactly that – cutting future benefits for 1.5 million veterans with tinnitus and sleep apnea to cover the cost of benefits for other veterans. Those savings do not come from waste or bureaucracy. They come directly from veterans.

The bill also contains large fee increases to VA home loan programs that veterans rely on for their housing needs. Ultimately, this bill would slash approximately $57 billion over the next 10 years in veterans earned disability compensation and $4 billion in home loan benefits and is opposed by a wide coalition – including several Veteran Service Organizations. 

House Democrats have filed a bipartisan discharge petition that would bring the Major Richard Star Act to the Floor, which would ensure medically retired veterans receive their full Department of Defense retirement pay and their full VA disability pay without cutting the benefits of other veterans. House Republicans should join this effort to advance the needs of the veteran community – without harming it in the process. 

Ranking Member Takano strongly opposes this bill.

The Rule, which was adopted on Tuesday, provides for one hour of debate equally divided and controlled by the Chair and Ranking Member of the Committee on Veterans' Affairs or their respective designees.

Possible Postponed Suspension (1 vote)

  1. H.R. 5362 – To name the VA clinic in Marietta, GA, as the “Colonel Michael H. Boyce Department of Veterans Affairs Multispecialty Clinic" (Rep. Loudermilk – Veterans' Affairs)

items.title: National Security, Department of State, and Related Programs Appropriations Act, 2027 (Rep. Díaz-Balart – Appropriations) (Subject to a Rule)
items.rawText: H.R. 8595 – National Security, Department of State, and Related Programs Appropriations Act, 2027 (Rep. Díaz-Balart – Appropriations) (Subject to a Rule) – VOTE NO

items.title: SAVE America Act will be added to H.R. 8595 upon passage of H.R. 8595 on the Floor.
items.rawText: Members are advised that the text of S. 1383 – SAVE America Act will be added to H.R. 8595 upon passage of H.R. 8595 on the Floor.

data.sourceText:
“One Minutes” (15 per side)

H.R. 8595 – National Security, Department of State, and Related Programs Appropriations Act, 2027 (Rep. Díaz-Balart – Appropriations) (Subject to a Rule) – VOTE NO

This bill contains $47.3 billion in funding, about 6 percent lower than the previous fiscal year and 20 percent lower than two years ago, further isolating America on the global stage at a time of international uncertainty and ongoing conflict in areas of strategic importance. While Republicans focus on defense alone, this bill undermines our most basic diplomatic and development capacities critical to national security.

Specifically, this bill includes no funding for the United Nations (UN) regular budget, as well as the UN Development Program, UN Women, UNICEF, and other critical multilateral institutions. It also cuts $20 million from educational and cultural exchange programs and $634 million from international financial institutions, such as the World Bank. Overall, this bill slashes $1.1 billion from contributions to international organizations – furthering a vacuum of leadership most easily filled by competitors and adversaries.

This bill also undermines ongoing efforts to bolster global health and combat the climate crisis. It includes $8.9 billion for global health programs, a decrease of $532 million from the previous fiscal year, and cuts millions from global health security, bilateral family planning, and HIV/AIDS prevention. Additionally, this bill prohibits funding for the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA), which provides health services to women in some of the most difficult to reach areas of the globe. It also prohibits funding for the Green Climate Fund, the Clean Technology Fund, and implementation of the Paris Agreement – preventing the United States from taking the lead on a holistic strategy to combat one of the world’s greatest existential threats.

House Republicans have also included harmful policy riders, including provisions that expand the global gag rule to further restrict support for women and LGBTQ+ individuals, prohibit certain special envoys – including the Special Envoy for Global Youth Issues, Special Envoy for Afghan Women and Girls, and Special Envoy for the Human Rights of LGBTQI+ Persons – from receiving funding, and ban funds for organizations that provide services or affirm the very existence of transgender youth.

Ranking Member DeLauro and Subcommittee Ranking Member Frankel oppose this bill.

The Rule, which was adopted yesterday, provides for one hour of general debate equally divided and controlled by the Chair and Ranking Member of the Committee on Appropriations or their respective designees and makes in order 29 amendments. Members may track consideration of amendments HERE.

Members are advised that the text of S. 1383 – SAVE America Act will be added to H.R. 8595 upon passage of H.R. 8595 on the Floor.

Suspensions (5 bills)

  1. H.R. 6007 – To name the Department of Veterans Affairs community-based outpatient clinic in Newton, New Jersey, as the “Anthony 'Tony' J. Gallopo VA Clinic" (Rep. Gottheimer – Veterans' Affairs)
  2. H.R. 5362 – To name the VA clinic in Marietta, GA, as the “Colonel Michael H. Boyce Department of Veterans Affairs Multispecialty Clinic" (Rep. Loudermilk – Veterans' Affairs)
  3. H.R. 6963 – To designate the community-based outpatient clinic of the Department of Veterans Affairs in Lafayette, Louisiana, as the “Rodney C. Hamilton Sr. VA Clinic” (Rep. Higgins – Veterans' Affairs)
  4. H.R. 4647 – Captain Paul W. ‘Bud’ Bucha VA Medical Center Act of 2025 (Rep. DeLauro – Veterans' Affairs)
  5. H.R. 4288 – To name the Department of Veterans Affairs community-based outpatient clinic in San Jose, California, as the “Corporal Patrick D. Tillman VA Clinic" (Rep. Panetta – Veterans' Affairs)
